quality.rationale: 基于真空爱因斯坦场方程的多步黎曼几何推导，完整还原Kasner各向异性宇宙模型及体积线性演化规律

origin.question:
Problem 19.41. Consider the homogeneous, anisotropic cosmological model with metric
$$
ds^{2} = - d t^{2} + g_{i j} (t) d x^{i} d x^{j}.
$$
where the space slices $t =$ constant have a flat geometry. Find the evolution of the $g_{ij}$ when the universe is "gravitation dominated," i.e. set $T^{\mu 
u}$ to zero in the field equations. Show that the volume of the universe goes to zero linearly with $t$ as $t 	o 0$ (in contrast to the $t^{3/2}$ or $t^2$ behavior for radiation- or matter-dominated Friedmann models).
origin.answer: $ds^2 = -dt^2 + t^{2P_1}dx^2 + t^{2P_2}dy^2 + t^{2P_3}dz^2$ (subject to $\\sum_i P_i = 1$ and $\\sum_i P_i^2 = 1$); volume $V \\propto t$
origin.solution:
Solution 19.41. The quickest way to compute the Ricci tensor for this metric is to use the formulas of Problem 9.33. We have
$$
\mathrm {K}_{\mathrm {i j}} = - \frac {1}{2} \dot {\mathrm {g}}_{\mathrm {i j}} \tag {1}
$$
$$
0 = \mathrm {R}_{\mathrm {t}}^{\mathrm {t}} = \mathrm {K}_{\mathrm {i j}} \mathrm {K}^{\mathrm {i j}} - \dot {\mathrm {K}} \tag {2}
$$
$$
0 = \mathrm {R}_{\mathrm {j}}^{\mathrm {t}} = \mathrm {K}_{\mathrm {j}, \mathrm {i}}^{\mathrm {i}} - \mathrm {K}, \mathrm {j} \tag {3}
$$
$$
0 = \mathrm {R}_{\mathrm {j}}^{\mathrm {i}} = \mathrm {K}_{\mathrm {j}}^{\mathrm {i}} \mathrm {K} - \dot {\mathrm {K}}_{\mathrm {j}}^{\mathrm {i}} \tag {4}
$$
where a dot denotes $\mathrm{d} / \mathrm{dt}$ . We have set $^{(3)}\mathbb{R}_{ij}$ to zero in Equation (4) because the 3-geometry is flat, and we have replaced covariant derivatives by partial derivatives in Equation (3).
Contracting Equation (4) gives $\mathbf{K} = \mathbf{K}^2$ and hence
$$
\mathrm {K} = - 1 / \mathrm {t} \tag {5}
$$
with a suitable choice of the constant of integration. Substituting Equation (5) back into Equation (4) gives
$$
\dot {\mathbf {K}}_{j}^{i} = - \mathbf {K}_{j}^{i} / t
$$
and hence
$$
\mathrm {K}_{\mathrm {j}}^{\mathrm {i}} = \mathrm {A}_{\mathrm {j}}^{\mathrm {i}} / \mathrm {t} \tag {6}
$$
where $\mathbf{A}_{\mathbf{j}}^{\mathbf{i}}$ is a constant matrix. We now choose the coordinates so that $\mathbf{K}_{\mathbf{j}}^{\mathbf{i}}$ is diagonal at some time $t = t_0$ . Then by Equation (6) $\mathbf{K}_{\mathbf{j}}^{\mathbf{i}}$ remains diagonal and can be written in the form
$$
\mathrm {K}_{\mathrm {j}}^{\mathrm {i}} = - \delta_{\mathrm {j}}^{\mathrm {i}} \mathrm {P}_{\mathrm {j}} / \mathrm {t} \tag {7}
$$
where $\mathbf{P_j} =$ constant. For this equation to be consistent with Equation (5) we must have
$$
\sum_{i} P_{i} = 1. \tag {8}
$$
Equation (3) is automatically satisfied, Equation (2) implies
$$
\sum_{i} P_{i}^{2} = 1 \tag {9}
$$
and Equation (1) becomes
$$
\dot {\mathbf {g}}_{i j} = - 2 \mathbf {g}_{i m} \mathbf {K}_{j}^{m} = 2 \mathbf {g}_{i j} \mathbf {P}_{j} / t
$$
and implies that
$$
\mathbf {g}_{\mathrm {i j}} = \delta_{\mathrm {i j}} \mathbf {t}^{2 \mathbf {P}_{\mathrm {i}}}
$$
Thus the final form of the metric ("Kasner metric") is
$$
ds^{2} = - d t^{2} + t^{2 P_{1}} d x^{2} + t^{2 P_{2}} d y^{2} + t^{2 P_{3}} d z^{2} \tag {10}
$$
subject to the constraints of Equations (8) and (9).
The volume of this universe is proportional to
$$
\left[^{(3)} \mathbf {g}_{\mathbf {i j}} \right]^{\frac {1}{2}} = \left(t^{2 \sum \mathbf {P}_{\mathbf {i}}}\right)^{\frac {1}{2}} = t
$$
which can be contrasted with the $t^{\frac{3}{2}}$ behavior of the radiation-dominated Friedmann model.
A convenient way of representing the constraints of Equations (8) and (9) is to draw a circle of radius 2/3 centered at $y = 1/3$ and to inscribe an equilateral triangle. The y-coordinates of the vertices of the triangle will satisfy Equations (8) and (9). From this we see that, except for the special case $(1,0,0)$ which can easily be shown to represent flat space, two of the P's lie between 0 and 1 and one P lies between 0 and -1/3. Thus space must contract along one axis while expanding along the other two.
